critic_raw:
The experiment uses a stratified random split at the image level for histopathological slides from a single academic hospital over three years. Pathology slides from the same patient are almost certainly present multiple times in this dataset — biopsies at different tumor stages or from multiple regions generate multiple slides. A patient-level random split would be required to prevent the model from exploiting patient-specific tissue characteristics (staining batch, section thickness, tissue processing artifacts) rather than learning subtype morphology. The design does not mention patient-level deduplication or patient-level train/test assignment.

Accuracy is selected as the primary metric for five-class imbalanced classification. In histopathology cancer subtyping, class frequencies are inherently unequal (some subtypes are rare). A model that achieves high accuracy by correctly classifying the two most common subtypes while entirely missing rare subtypes satisfies the primary metric while failing clinically. The design mentions F1 as secondary but does not specify macro vs. weighted F1, and model selection is guided by accuracy — guaranteeing that rare-class performance is not prioritized during training.

The CNN uses transfer learning from pretrained models (unspecified), while the SVM baseline receives standard features. The choice of pretrained model source — ImageNet-pretrained, pathology-specific pretrained (PathAI, PLIP), or self-supervised — is not stated. ImageNet pretrained models carry visual priors optimized for natural scenes (edges, textures at macro scales), which may not align with microscopy feature scales. A pathology-pretrained backbone provides a structural advantage that is separate from the CNN architecture's merit.

Staining variation is mentioned as a preprocessing concern, but the normalization strategy is not specified. Macenko normalization, Vahadane normalization, and simple RGB standardization produce substantially different results on H&E slides. Stain normalization fitted on training data and applied to test data is the correct approach, but the normalization algorithm choice affects inter-hospital generalizability substantially — and this is a single-hospital dataset being evaluated on a holdout from the same hospital, so stain distribution is already narrower than real deployment.

The design does not specify the magnification level at which CNN features are extracted. Breast cancer subtype classification requires different magnification levels for different subtypes: lobular carcinoma is recognizable at 10x, while DCIS requires 20-40x to identify cribriform patterns. A single-magnification evaluation misses multi-scale pathology that is essential for subtype discrimination.
all_issues_raised: Patient-level leakage: multiple slides from the same patient (different biopsies, tumor regions) are almost certainly present in the dataset, and image-level random splitting allows patient-specific tissue artifacts (staining batch, section thickness) to leak from training to test evaluation.

Accuracy as the primary metric and model selection criterion for five-class imbalanced subtype classification will optimize for majority-class performance, potentially ignoring rare subtypes that are clinically critical.

The pretrained backbone source is unspecified — a pathology-specific pretrained model (PLIP, UNI) provides a substantial structural advantage over ImageNet pretraining that is independent of CNN architecture merit, confounding the comparison with the SVM baseline.

Stain normalization algorithm is unspecified; Macenko, Vahadane, and RGB standardization produce substantially different tissue appearances, and this single-hospital dataset does not test generalization to new staining batches.

Magnification level for CNN feature extraction is unspecified; breast cancer subtype discrimination requires multi-scale analysis, and single-magnification evaluation misses morphological features that define specific subtypes.
